data.title: Sprouts Farmers Market: Moving Fast In The National Grocery Business
data.description:
Sprouts (NASDAQ:SFM) is all about being a "healthy, instantly fresh, and affordable foods provider." Its thematic old country store appeals to customers against the massive techno-machine that rules the future of legendary food chains.

But Sprouts also embraces tech adaptations. Amazon (AMZN) engulfs online food shopping, so possible rivals with big eyes have to think twice before going to war for a dominant online market share against the giant of all online commerce.

Today, the supermarket business resembles a massive souk with vendors screaming out deals daily to consumers sifting for the cheapest prices. No major food chain today positions itself as anything but a merchant with a tent and cliffs of merchandise hawking away at the passing crowds. They offer no distinctive market positions against one another other than the obvious advantages of location.

Because of this, Sprouts may be one of the best dollar-for-dollar buys in the sector today.

Its unique selling proposition is the ultimate extension of a country store shopping experience and merchandise that appeals to what I view as a sea change. Its great success is a reflection of value to rediscovered instincts about the cultural and practical roles in newer generations.

And that, believe it or not, is the great opportunity presented by the shares of Sprouts. I think the irony works this way on this stock. The quality of its performance has brought expectations close to its current pricing. And yet, many analysts have taken the stance that while it's a great stock, it has been traded to its real value and no future is strong enough to warrant opening a position even at $105.

Consensus is wrong.

Sprouts, more than anything, is one of the new ways the public has been embracing brands with an old family-style touch. It's become part of our new ethos. Denizens from the alphabet generations have brought a new embrace of the traditional nostalgia marketing approaches.

Sprouts ($105.31 at writing, last high Feb. 23, 2024, $177.12) is not about shopping; it's about a retro psychology that routes back to earlier days as shopping was at the turn of the last century.

It's the old-time country store experience craved by more consumers every day.

Bottom line for me: Sprouts is a solid performer with a top ceiling ahead as the evidence of a simplified society emerges from the high-tech world.

Q2 Solid performance: Q2 highlights:

Revenue: $2.2 billion, beats by $30 million.

Diluted EPS: $1.35, beats by $.12.

Sales up 18%.

Same-store growth up 8%.

Net sales growth: 14.4% to $16.

Bears do not see a continuing growth arc for the stock. They see a solid stock fully valued with few elements that suggest it's stagnant for any future higher valuation implicit in its business. This is patently wrong.

As an investor always on the hunt for low-risk, strong forward momentum stocks hiding in their sector of giants, to pass this up as a buy-on-the-dip move seems contrary to common sense.

Understand now that the consumer motivations that have driven the company to a solid performance still exist. Whether buried inside the change of ethos since COVID or just on the basis of delivering the goods on growth and earnings, passing up Sprouts now is a huge mistake.

Organic growth includes an expansion of stores from the current 400 to an eventual 1,400. If we assume the new stores over time will perform at a comparable level to the existing ones, how can you pass on this stock now?

data.title: Xior: High-Yielding Student Housing REIT Is Now Finally Attractive
data.description:
Introduction

Xior (OTCPK:XIORF) is a Belgian REIT focusing on a specific sub-segment of (semi-) residential real estate. The REIT is one of the largest entities in Europe focusing on student housing and is active in excess of 40 cities in eight countries. About 45% of its assets (measured by fair value) are located in The Netherlands, followed by Belgium (17%) and Spain (11%), which all adds to the diversification.

Xior has its main listing in Belgium, where it's trading on Euronext Brussels with XIOR as its ticker symbol. There are currently 46.7M shares outstanding (and this includes the impact of the recent dividend payment, as almost half of the dividend rights were converted into new shares), and at the current share price of 28.60 EUR, this represents a market capitalization of approximately 1.34B EUR. The average daily trading volume on Xior’s primary listing is 49,000 shares per day. I will use the Euro as base currency throughout this article, unless indicated otherwise.

A robust result, and the stock is now finally attractively priced

As you can see below, Xior reported a total net rental result of approximately 86.6M EUR, and this helped to push the total property result (comparable to the NOI) to approximately 92.2M EUR. And as the income statement below shows, Xior also incurred approximately 4M EUR in technical costs, resulting in a pretty high margin.

It doesn’t make much sense to have a look at a REIT by discussing the income statement, as the EPRA earnings (comparable to the FFO) provide a much better metric to compare real estate companies. As the image below shows, the starting point of the EPRA earnings calculation is the almost 66M EUR in net income, but this included a 56M EUR contribution from the change in the fair value of the real estate properties. The EPRA earnings in the second quarter came in at approximately 46.6M EUR, for an EPRA EPS of 1.02 EUR.

After taking the IFRIC 21 adjustments into consideration (IFRIC 21 arranges how to record a liability related to a government levy), the underlying IFRIC 21 adjusted EPS was 1.10 EUR. And this puts Xior nicely on track to meet (and perhaps exceed) its full-year guidance of 2.21 EUR per share. As Xior maintains an anticipated payout ratio of approximately 80%, the REIT expects to propose a dividend of 1.768 EUR per share. This represents a dividend yield of approximately 6% (the dividend is, of course, subject to the standard 30% dividend withholding tax in Belgium). While that’s an attractive yield (especially given the 80% payout ratio), it also makes sense to have a look at the balance sheet.

Earlier this year, Xior had to strengthen its balance sheet, and the issue was solved by issuing more shares. As this happened below the NAV per share, there was a small dilutive effect, but as you can see below, the NTA (net tangible assets) and NDV (net disposable value) were still almost 39 EUR per share. And that’s more than 30% higher than the current share price.

There is, however, one caveat: The loan-to-value ratio remains relatively high. As you can see below, the total LTV ratio at the end of June was almost 50%, excluding the real estate transfer tax, and approximately 46.4%, including the impact of a 200M EUR real estate transfer tax.

It’s important to emphasize the fair value of the real estate assets was based on an average rental yield of 5.89%.

I anticipate the earnings to continue to increase in 2026 and 2027, as Xior will add approximately 1,500 units within the next few quarters.

The remaining capex to completion is just 24M EUR, while these will add about 13M EUR to the rental income on an annual basis. This will likely add about 0.20-0.25 EUR per share to the EPRA earnings, and this should put Xior in a position to achieve a run rate of close to 2.50 EUR per share by the end of next year.

Investment thesis

I have never really been interested in Xior, as A) I was not quite sure if parents or students would continue to pay a lot of money for student housing (I was wrong, as Xior continues to report strong retention rates and continued demand), and B) the stock has historically been very expensive. Just to give you an idea, when the stock was trading in the 50 EUR range in the summer of 2021, this represented an EPRA earnings multiple of almost 30.

That was not a price I was willing to pay, but I'm getting increasingly interested at the current share price level. The stock is trading at a 40% lower share price since the summer of 2021, while the EPRA EPS is approximately 25% higher, resulting in an earnings multiple of just 13.

I will keep an eye on Xior’s LTV ratio, as I think that's the weakest element and certainly a factor to follow up on. But as Xior retains 20% of its earnings and has been able to convince its shareholder base to convert half of the dividend rights into new stock, the LTV ratio could decrease by 177 bps per year (assuming the value of the real estate remains unchanged).

I currently have no position in Xior, but I will likely initiate a long position in the next few days.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: REITs On Clearance In A Rate Cut World: Here's 2 With Prices That Are Right
data.description:
Introduction

Growing up my family and I watched a lot of the TV show, The Price is Right. If you're older, then it's likely you're familiar with the show hosted by the legendary Bob Barker.

With contestants competing for prizes by guessing the correct prices of items, the current macro environment made me think of the once beloved show.

The market continues to make new highs, even with the seemingly large amount of bad news. And it seems like a large pullback or market correction is inevitable with how the market has performed.

But not one to try to time the market or make predictions, this brought me to two, high-quality REITs whose current prices seem right.

Honestly, the entire REIT (XLRE) sector seems undervalued when compared to the overall market. And currently it presents some attractive buying opportunities if you're a long-term investors.

However, I wanted to highlight two, high-quality REITs that not only are likely to continue providing stable income, but should see some solid price appreciation in the next year or two.

Why REITs?

Many investors have been turned off by REITs. Especially, over the past 3 years as many have underperformed. And most know this is a result of high-interest rates.

And while rates are still historically high, multiple rate cuts are expected. This is likely to provide tailwinds going forward, resulting in strong price & total returns.

With the FED already slashing rates by 25 basis points in September, many are still down in recent months. But I expect this to change starting in the beginning of 2026. Especially, if we see multiple rate cuts as expected.

The reason why REITs are still underperforming in 2025 despite rates being lower has nothing to do with their fundamentals. But it has more so to do with fixed-rate investments.

REITs are known for providing stable income. But investors are able to currently get just as high, if not higher, yields in fixed-rate investments like high-yield savings, money market accounts, or certificates of deposits.

If you could get over 4% with no risk, would you put your money in the stock market and risk losing capital? I'm currently getting 3.8% in my high-yield savings. But there are some that are even higher.

The chart below shows the average regular savings rate vs the average high-yield savings rate for the month of October. While this has come down due to interest rates, investors are still able to get a solid yield above 4%.

But I think once those rates hit around 3% or lower, the market will see an influx of money coming back into the market. And investors will look for potential bargains with higher yields. As a result, REITs will likely benefit since many have yields above 3%.

And the high-quality ones will see strong capital appreciation. Below is the inflow to money market funds in the past 5 years. It should be a no-brainer that this saw a dramatic increase around 2022/2023.

As the FED hiked interest rates their fastest in U.S. history, fixed-rate investments became more attractive. As a result, REITs suffered. But for investors like me, this presented a buying opportunity. Especially, since many REITs were considered overvalued going into COVID.

Agree Realty (ADC) and Realty Income (O), two well-known net lease REITs, both traded near $80 in the last quarter of 2019. While Realty Income has continued to underperform, Agree Realty currently trades around the price they did in early 2020. They also reached a new 52-week high back in April.

I expect Agree Realty to see some strong price appreciation. However, I think Realty Income may continue to lag in share price. But these aren't the two REITs I want to talk about today. Without further ado, let's get into it.

#1 Essential Properties Realty Trust (EPRT)

Last month I wrote an in-depth article on EPRT discussing how I thought they presented an attractive buying opportunity. The REIT has long traded at a premium despite being lesser-known vs peers like ADC & O.

And this says a lot about their fundamentals and business model. Unlike Agree Realty and Realty Income, who focus on investment-rated retailers, ERPT does the opposite. While this could make them riskier if we see a severe recession, the REIT is able to achieve more attractive spreads, leading to AFFO accretion and growth.

They are also able to get tenants to agree to things like master leases and longer leases. This gives them more predictable cash flows. Master leases also make them less likely to see rent defaults, as tenants would default on multiple properties instead of justone.

This is why they currently have one of the highest occupancy ratings in the sector at 99.6% despite the tough macro environment. For comparison, this is the same as Agree Realty's 99.6% and higher than Realty Income's 98.6%, a testament to EPRT's fundamentals.

Essential Properties Realty Trust also has one of the lowest-leveraged balance sheets in the sector, with a net debt-to-EBITDA ratio of 3.5x. They also have no debt maturing until 2027. This positions them favorably putting for a lower rate environment.

Although REITs have dealt with some spread compression, lower interest rates should provide tailwinds in this area too. This coupled with lower rates on fixed-rate investments, should result in REITs seeing strong upside.

And ERPT's current dividend yield of over 4% also makes them a worthy option. Moreover, if EPRT can return to its normal multiple, which I think they can, they offer double-digit upside to their price target near $40 by the end of 2027.

#2 NNN REIT (NNN)

NNN is another REIT I covered last month. And despite raising guidance amidst uncertainty and tenant headwinds, their share price has remained somewhat flat, up less than 1%. NNN has a lot of similar qualities to EPRT, or should I say EPRT is similar to NNN since the latter has a much longer track record.

NNN REIT has 36 years of dividend increases, making them a viable long-term option. This is also the third-longest streak among publicly-listed REITs. Having been through multiple downturns, I think it's safe to say NNN REIT isn't going anywhere anytime soon.

They also focus on non-rated tenants, and this makes them less appealing to some investors. But they have continued to perform solidly amidst uncertainty. This is shown by their strong occupancy rating, cash flows, and experienced management team.

So, if the REIT is so fundamentally sound, why has volatility picked up?

If you're asking yourself this question, it's because occupancy has fallen to its lowest level since 2021. A time when this fell to 97.9%. NNN's current occupancy sits at 98%, falling from 98.5% in 2024 and 99.5% in 2023.

As previously mentioned, NNN focuses on non-rated tenants, and the current macro environment has presented challenges. This is evident by the bankruptcy filings discussed in last month's article, so I won't go into depth.

But I remain confident in NNN's ability to continue rewarding shareholders with reliable & stable dividends. And as occupancy slowly picks up, likely over the next year or two, so should the REIT's share price.

NNN REIT is also known for having one of the most conservative payout ratios in the sector, typically around 70%. This gives them ample retained cash flow for investment opportunities and leeway should occupancy continue to decline and dividend sustainability become impacted.

Their balance sheet was also strong. Although, they do have some upcoming debt maturing with $400 million next month. But liquidity was plentiful with $1.4 billion available and retained cash flow of $50 million after their last quarterly dividend.

Although I expect limited upside near-term and them less likely to see strong price appreciation because of current tenant headwinds, investors collect a dividend yield above 5% while they wait.

I expect lower interest rates to provide tailwinds and occupancy to gradually increase over the next year or two. If NNN returns to its normal multiple, investors can see double-digit upside to their price target of near $57 by the end of 2027.

Risks & Conclusion

Although I consider both Essential Properties Realty Trust and NNN REIT to be of high-quality, their portfolios make them riskier.

As a result, both could see additional downside if the economy continues to weaken or we see a recession. If so, occupancy is likely to tick up. This could likely lead to a pullback in their share prices.

Investors should keep a close eye on this in the coming quarters. Especially, with NNN REIT, as this is the lowest level they've seen since 2012. But I expect their dividend yields of 4% and above 5%, respectively, to remain safe.

Because of their price pullbacks and ability to provide income, both EPRT and NNN REIT are attractive buying opportunities for the long-term.

data.title: JPMorgan, Goldman Sachs Among Big Banks Set To Report Earnings Next Week
data.description:
Get ahead of the market by subscribing to Seeking Alpha's Wall Street Week Ahead, a preview of key events scheduled for the coming week. The newsletter keeps you informed of the biggest stories set to make headlines, including upcoming IPOs, investor days, earnings reports, and conference presentations.

Stock index futures edged higher on Friday, following a pause in Wall Street's rally as concerns grew over the ongoing U.S. government shutdown. On Thursday, major indexes ended lower as momentum faded. The dip came after the U.S. Senate failed to pass two bills aimed at ending the shutdown. Adding to market uncertainty, Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell refrained from offering any insights into the economic outlook or monetary policy during a Fed event. Traders are now increasingly worried that if the shutdown does persist into next week, they won't get the CPI release on Wednesday, which will leave them more uncertain about the U.S. economy.

Amid a U.S. government shutdown, investors will keep a close eye on economic reports that will be released next week; however, government numbers are in doubt as the shutdown continues. The economic reports that are expected to be announced next week include Construction Spending and Wholesale Trade Sales on Monday, with the NFIB Small Business Optimism Index and Redbook Index on Tuesday. Wednesday will include reports on MBA Mortgage Applications and the Beige Book, with the Philadelphia Fed Manufacturing Index to be released on Thursday.

JPMorgan (JPM), Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), Wells Fargo (WFC), Goldman Sachs (GS), BlackRock (BLK), Citigroup (C), Bank of America (BAC), Morgan Stanley (MS), Abbott Labs (ABT), American Express (AXP), and State Street (STT) will report earnings next week.

_____________________________________________________________________

Earnings spotlight: Monday, October 13: Fastenal (FAST). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Tuesday, October 14: JPMorgan, J&J, Wells Fargo, Goldman Sachs, BlackRock, Citigroup. See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Wednesday, October 15: Bank of America, Morgan Stanley (MS), Abbott Labs. See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Thursday, October 16: Charles Schwab (SCHW), Bank of NY Mellon (BK), U.S. Bancorp (USB). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Friday, October 17: American Express, State Street. See the full earnings calendar.

Volatility watch: Rigetti Computing (RGTI) and USA Rare Earth (USAR) are set up for a volatile week of trading based on options volume. The most overbought stocks per their 14-day relative strength index include Churchill Capital Corp. X (CCCX), GigCapital7 (GIG), and Nuburu (BURU). The most oversold stocks per their 14-day relative strength index include Stellus Capital (SCM), TaskUs (TASK), and Great Elm Capital (GECC).

IPO watch: HW Electro KK (HWEP) is expected to price its IPO and begin to trade during the week. The quiet periods expire on StubHub (STUB), Netskope (NTSK), WaterBridge Infrastructure (WBI), Pattern Group (PTRN), and ChowChow Cloud International (CHOW) to free up analysts to post ratings. The IPO lockup period expires for certain blocks of shares of Chagee Holdings (CHA).

JPMorgan Earnings Preview: JPMorgan is set to announce third-quarter earnings next week, with analysts expecting the banking giant to report earnings per share of $4.87 on a revenue of $45.57B. Analysts expect continued outperformance amid a strong track record of beating estimates. JPM's investment thesis centers on robust credit card growth, resilient investment banking momentum, and management's guidance for higher net interest income in FY 2025. Given the weakening labor market, investors need to carefully monitor the management commentary on the state of the US consumer. At the same time, the progress made by the company on growing its credit card business would also be a key indicator of how the US consumer has been during this uncertain macroeconomic backdrop.

Goldman Sachs Earnings Preview: Banking giant Goldman Sachs is scheduled to report its third-quarter earnings next week, and analysts expect the company to report earnings per share of $10.62 on a revenue of $14.13B. GS' investment banking and FICC financing segments are showing robust growth, while its Asset & Wealth Management business is expanding, especially in alternative assets. Upcoming Q3 earnings are expected to reflect continued positive trends, but recent strong stock performance may lead to near-term price consolidation.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: DraftKings' Meltdown Is Here - Prediction Market Prospects Remain Mixed
data.description:
DKNG's Much-Needed Correction Is Here, As It Enters My Buy Zones

I previously covered DraftKings (NASDAQ:DKNG) in July 2025, discussing how the lowered FY2025 guidance had been similarly observed in its direct peer, Flutter Entertainment's (FLUT) FanDuel, thanks to the "adverse March Madness sports results."

Despite the ongoing regulatory headwinds observed in the gaming industry, I had been convinced by the company's growing bottom lines, the robust performance metrics in FQ1'25, and the enviable position as the second largest iGaming player in the US.

DKNG 1Y Stock Price

Since that reiterated Buy rating upon a moderate retracement to the established 2024/ 2025 trading support at $33s ranges, it is apparent that the bears remain in the driver's seat, with DKNG experiencing a deep meltdown by -23.7% compared to the wider market at +7%.

A similar, albeit less severe correction trend, has been observed in FLUT at -12.4%, attributed to the market's overreaction to the potential sports betting competition arising from the prediction markets.

For reference, the prediction market has been increasingly popular after the last US presidential election, when Polymarket reported up to $2.3B in trading volume in October 2024 and another $2.6B in November 2024, "thanks to a blitz of activity around the U.S. presidential election."

Another popular prediction market platform, Kalshi, has also been gaining momentum as the company reports new trading records of over $275M during the college and pro football game session, well exceeding that of the 2024 US election at $245M.

While prediction markets are typically used "to take a position on the outcome of real-world events, such as inflation rates, election results, or weather patterns," market analysts already expect the two start-ups to pull "away between 5% and 10% from the sports betting sector."

The latter is unsurprising indeed, since the prediction market appears to be legal in certain US states where sports betting has not been approved, allowing the platforms and users to bypass the regulatory requirement while presenting growth risks to legacy players like FLUT and DKNG, with the latter two likely to experience mindshare and market share erosion moving forward.

With FLUT also partnering with CME Group (CME) to enter the event-based prediction markets and DKNG yet to confirm (or deny) the prior rumor surrounding the acquisition of another prediction market platform, Railbird, I can understand why the market has decided to de-risk and sell first, with it somewhat reasonable that DKNG has been worst affected by the meltdown.

On the other hand, readers may want to note that certain US states and courts have had mixed resolutions surrounding Kalshi's legal ability to continue offering sports betting without license, with it remaining to be seen how the legal stance against the prediction platform and its license partners may develop moving forward.

DKNG's iCasino Market Share

While the path to legal clarity may be prolonged, I maintain my conviction surrounding DKNG's highly strategic position as the second largest player with 23.9% in market share as of August 2025, despite the ongoing divergence against FLUT since August 2024.

Growing iGaming & Online Sports Betting Revenues

This is especially since the American Gaming Association is already reporting growing iGaming and Online Sports Betting [OSB] revenues in the US, with the online spending share of the overall US gaming revenues expanding to 33.5% by Q2'25, nearly tripling compared to the 13% reported in Q2'21.

The same robust growth has been observed in the higher iGaming and OSB revenues of $6.52B in Q2'25 (+27% YoY), with June 2025 bringing forth notably richer growth profile for iGaming by +31.1% YoY and sports betting by +39.5% YoY to an overall monthly revenue of $6.4B.

These paint a promising picture indeed, with it naturally underscoring why the two market leaders, DKNG included, are likely to continue reporting robust top-line performances moving forward.

This is albeit with the likely to be lumpy bottom-line performances as observed in the customer-friendly sport outcomes in the 2024-2025 NFL season, March 2025, and during the ongoing September 2025 NFL season.

Even so, DKNG has hedged its growth prospects through the strategic/ exclusive advertising deal with NBCUniversal (CMCSA), with the multi-year agreement allowing the former to be directly integrated into the latter's sports events across the linear, streaming, and digital ecosystems.

This is a highly strategic move indeed, since eMarketer already expects more US sports viewers to choose the digital platform moving forward, with 2027 expected to bring forth 127.4M of viewers in the US (expanding at a 3Y CAGR of +6.5% from 2024 levels of 105.3M).

Combined with 55% of American adults already participating in gambling activities in 2024, compared to the 43% reported in 2019, I believe that DKNG's new partnership may potentially drive renewed growth opportunities across the market share and top/ bottom-line performances moving forward as a pure play iGaming company.

A similar strategy has been observed with PENN Entertainment (PENN), through the partnership with Disney (DIS) and the latter's expanded D2C offerings across Hulu/ ESPN/ NFL/ WWE, amongst others, with it likely to trigger both parties' easier user acquisition trends and expanded cross-selling of the ESPN Bet's sports betting offerings.

The Consensus Forward Estimates

These reasons may also be why the consensus forward estimates remain rather promising despite the ongoing downgrades, with DKNG still expected to report an excellent top/ bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +22.3%/ +125.2% through FY2027.

This is compared to the prior estimates of +23.2%/ +149.7% a year ago, while remaining highly profitable after numerous years of cash burn and turning the leaf in FY2024.

So, Is DKNG Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

DKNG 5Y Stock Price

For now, DKNG's pullback has brought it directly to the established uptrend support line since July 2023, with it already breaching numerous established support lines while trading well below the 50/ 100/ 200 day moving averages.

DKNG's Mixed Technical Indicators

The same mixed technical indicators have been observed in DKNG's oversold Relative Strength Index [RSI] and reasonable trading volumes, against that in early 2022, with it remaining to be seen if the bulls may defend these levels and trigger the next cycle of upward momentum.

DKNG Valuations

Here is why I believe opportunistic investors may consider adding, since DKNG has finally retraced to my Buy Zone at the established 2024/ 2025 trading support at $33s while appearing to be relatively attractive at my estimated P/E non-GAAP valuations of 28.31x.

This is based on the current stock prices of $33.98 and the annualized FQ2'25 adj EPS performance of $1.20 (+528.5% QoQ/ +150% YoY).

Combined with the consensus 3Y EPS growth estimates at a CAGR of +125.2%, it is apparent that DKNG remains cheaply valued at FWD PEG ratio of 0.22x

When compared to:

  • FLUT at FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 29.03x and FWD PEG non-GAAP ratio of 0.48x,
  • PENN at 48.29x/ NA, and
  • the sector median at 17.86x/ 1.78x, respectively,

DKNG's recent meltdown by -28.8% from August 2025 heights has been a boon for those looking to add, especially given its strategic positioning in the iGaming segment, the growing partnership, and the market's long-term tailwinds, significantly aided by the prediction market's mixed legal prospects.

Combined with the stable balance sheet health on a YoY basis, the reiterated FY2025 guidance in the last FQ2'25 earnings call, and the improved margin of safety arising from the much-needed deep pullback, I am maintaining my Buy rating here.

Do not miss this dip.

data.title: BUI: Great Long-Term Potential But Trades At A Premium
data.description:
Overview

BlackRock Utilities, Infrastructure & Power Opportunities Trust (NYSE:BUI) is structured as a closed end fund that aims to provide exposure to companies that are involved in utilities, infrastructure, and power-related operations. The fund has an inception dating back to 2011, which means we have over a decade of operating history to reference. Income funds like BUI can be an efficient way to hedge against the uncertainty and volatility that traditional equities may expose investors to.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that BUI's share price has increased by about 14.5%. The fund was able to participate in the market rally over the last few months. When including all distributions that were paid out, the total return jumps up to about 22.3% over the same time frame. BUI now offers investors a starting dividend yield of 6%, which makes it an enticing choice for investors seeking a source of high-income generation within their portfolio.

Due to the recent market rally, BUI now trades at a premium valuation. Despite this, the fund's structure and strategy have been proven to be effective over a long-term holding period. Additionally, it appears that the distributions are sustainable for now, which makes BUI a great option for investors that want to prioritize income reliability. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy that BUI implements to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fund overview, BUI has total managed assets of $608M that are spread across 51 different holdings. The fund has a management fee of 1%, which is reasonable considering the higher yield that is provided. Under normal market conditions, the fund aims to invest at least 80% of its assets into equities that are engaged in utilities, infrastructure, and power-related segments. Additionally, the fund offers some global exposure to equities outside of the US. The breakdown by region is as follows:

  • United States: 61.55%
  • France: 9.24%
  • United Kingdom: 5.33%
  • Italy: 4.39%
  • Canada: 3.95%
  • Germany: 3.91%
  • Denmark: 2.29%
  • Portugal: 2.29%
  • Belgium: 1.88%
  • Ireland: 1.76%

Looking at the breakdown by sector below, we can see that BUI is most heavily allocated to Utilities at 46.50% of assets. This is followed by exposure to Capital Goods and Energy investments, which account for 28.85% and 9.85% of assets, respectively.

As a result of the fund's focus, BUI instantly provides exposure to some of the highest quality companies that provide essential services across the globe. For instance, BUI's largest position is NextEra Energy (NEE) at 5.06% of assets. This is followed by other high quality companies such as Williams Companies (WMB), National Grid (NGG), Southern Company (SO), and Duke Energy (DUK), just to name a few. A notable trait of many of the top holdings is that these businesses tend to prioritize dividend stability for shareholders. For instance, here are some of the notable traits for BUI's top holdings:

  • Southern Company: 23 years of consecutive dividend raises without disruption.
  • Duke Energy: Currently has a reasonable payout ratio of 66.45%.
  • NextEra Energy: Maintains a five-year dividend CAGR of 10.20%.

Despite the diversified exposure to high quality businesses, I believe that investors should limit their expectations around the fund's ability to provide a meaningful level of capital appreciation. This can be attributed to the inclusion of an option writing strategy that is implemented as a way to amplify income generated from its holdings. According to the fact sheet, BUI writes options against 33.33% of its portfolio at this time. Option writing strategies cap the growth potential to the selected strike price, which translates to lower capital appreciation over time.

Just to demonstrate this weakness, I pulled in a performance chart over the last ten-year period. As we can see below, BUI's share price has increased by about 57.4% over the last ten years. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up above 210% over the same period. So while some capital appreciation may occur, investors should anticipate that most of their returns will come from the fund's ability to continue paying out large distributions.

Valuation & Risk Profile

Since BUI is structured as a closed-end fund, the price can trade at a difference from the actual value of its underlying assets. Following the recent market rally, BUI now trades at a small premium to NAV of approximately 2.9%. For reference, BUI has traded at an average premium to NAV of 0.14% over the last five year period. To better visualize the current premium in relation to BUI's historical range, I added the red line to the chart below. As we can see, BUI currently trades at the higher end of its pricing range over the last decade, which may be an indication that it isn't a good time to accumulate shares.

To get better clarity into the fund's operating performance, I reviewed the latest semi-annual report for 2025. Net investment income amounted to $0.15 per share, while net realized gains landed at $2.97 per share. Assuming a similar performance over the remainder of 2025, BUI is aligned to generate net investment income that is on par with prior years. However, the fund's net investment income is far below where it needs to be to support distributions. Therefore, the fund is reliant on net realized gains to bridge the gap between earnings and the distributions paid.

The challenging part is that net realized gains can only be captured when market momentum is positive, which allows BUI to sell securities at a higher valuation than they were purchased for. Additionally, the option premiums collected are also classified as net realized gains. Therefore, the fund doesn't offer any protection against market declines and unfavorable market environments can result in slower NAV growth. For reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts:

  • 2020: $23.80 per share
  • 2021: $25.86 per share
  • 2022: $22.37 per share
  • 2023: $22.53 per share
  • 2024: $22.65 per share
  • 2025 (semi-annual): $24.95 per share

The fund's strategy is also proven to be effective when comparing its performance to a more traditional ETF that tracks the utilities sector, such as the Utilities Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLU). As we can see below, BUI's total return outperforms XLU over the last ten year, despite the limited capital appreciation related to the inclusion of option writing. Therefore, BUI has proven to be a solid long-term holding for investors that prefer a buy-and-hold approach.

However, the fund's reliance on net realized gains makes it vulnerable to a scenario where markets pull back for a prolonged period of time. Since the fund can only support distributions when net realized gains are captured, the fund has the potential to be negatively impacted by a scenario where the utilities sector trends downward. This may result in the fund paying out more than it actually generates in income.

A great example of this can be demonstrated when measuring the performance throughout 2022's decline. Market indexes declined in response to record breaking inflationary data and aggressive interest rate hikes from the Fed. BUI was not able to provide resilience through this decline and saw its share price decline by nearly 22%, which was more than the S&P 500. Even when including all distributions that were paid out, the total loss amounted to 16.7% for 2022. Therefore, investors should understand that a higher yield doesn't automatically translate to a higher return.

Dividend Sustainability

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.136 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 6%. If we take this monthly distribution and multiply it by twelve months, we get an estimated annual payout amount of $1.632 per share. Referring back to the same semi-annual report for 2025, BUI has displayed the ability to consistently support the distributions that are paid. For reference, here are the annual earnings amounts over the last few years:

  • 2020: $3.23 per share
  • 2021: $3.51 per share
  • 2022: ($2.04) per share
  • 2023: $1.61 per share
  • 2024: $1.59 per share
  • 2025 (semi-annual): $3.12 per share

As we can see, BUI's earnings have been so strong that there were also instances where the income collected was capable of supporting multiple years' worth of payouts. For instance, BUI generated $3.51 per share in earnings in 2021, which was enough to support more than two years worth of distributions. Assuming a strong performance over the remainder of 2025, there is also the chance we see a similar story play out once more. As a result of this strong earnings consistency, we can see below that the fund has maintained steady payouts over the last decade.

So although the fund doesn't have the strongest dividend growth rate over the last ten years, payouts have remained incredibly consistent. This consistency must be commended because it allows investors to essentially create their own dividend growth by remaining invested and reinvesting dividends back into accumulating more shares.

However, it's worth noting that the distributions are typically fueled by the combination of net realized gains and net investment income. Therefore, the income received from BUI may not be tax-efficient. So based on your current tax bracket, the amount of capital invested, or desired income level, a position in BUI may be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account to offset these potential tax burdens.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I rate BUI as a hold at this time due to the premium valuation. Although the fund has a great track record of performance and supports the high distributions, it may not be the best time to accumulate shares. BUI is a great way to instantly gain exposure to some of the highest quality companies within the utilities and infrastructure sector. The dividends are well supported by earnings, and this makes BUI a great choice for investors seeking income stability. However, the distributions may not be the most tax-efficient choice for investors.

data.title: Build Your Income Portfolio With 7%+ Yields: RQI
data.description:
Co-authored by Treading Softly

Entropy, the slow decline from order into disorder that occurs over time, can be readily seen throughout human history. Often, businesses begin as small, agile entities that slowly transform into bureaucracy-laden monstrosities. To fight against this, we strive to streamline processes and bring back the agile nature that once existed. To do this, we seek one thing.

Efficiency – it's a word that people tend to think of favorably. Everyone wants things to be more "efficient," right? People say that, but the reality is that we revel in our inefficiencies. Odds are you live in a shelter that's way bigger than what you strictly need. Structures with "sleep pods" would be a far more efficient use of construction materials. Would you rather have a meal that consists of a nutrient bar with precisely the nutrients your body needs, or a home-cooked meal full of your favorite but inefficient (and often outright unhealthy) foods?

Many dystopian sci-fi novels and movies portray a world where the search for efficiency leads to some very unpleasant and inhumane results. I would argue that being efficient is very inhuman.

What about our investments? We want to be efficient with those, right? Sure. Being efficient sounds nice, but the reality is that our gains in the market often stem from the reality that the market isn't efficient. We don't have to be perfectly efficient to profit in the market.

One area where the market can be quite inefficient is in closed-end funds. CEFs are distinct from ETFs in one crucial way: Inefficient pricing. You see, ETFs have a mechanism to ensure that their price is trading very close to the underlying NAV. They might be off by a penny or two, but the price you pay when you buy an ETF will be very close to the price you would pay if you bought everything yourself.

With a CEF, the price trades independently. This means that it's up to the market to determine what the price is, and that price can be (and usually is) different than NAV.

The Market and NAV Inefficiency

Cohen & Steers Quality Income Realty Fund (NYSE:RQI), yielding around 7.9%, which we find to be a highly valuable holding, as we've mentioned previously. RQI is currently trading at about a 4% discount to NAV:

RQI provides our portfolio two things. The first is a portfolio of high-quality REITs. RQI's top 10 holdings reads like a REIT hall of fame list: Source

It's a fairly easy list to replicate, and with 48% of its portfolio in the top 10 holdings, this isn't exactly a CEF that would be difficult to duplicate. But if we can pick it up for an extra 4% discount, why pay full price? We get a discount, and we get a portfolio of unquestionably high-quality REITs.

The second thing that RQI provides is access to cheap leverage. Source

RQI currently leverages its portfolio at 29% of assets. This is a ballpark they tend to stick around. Their average cost of financing stands at 2.2%.

Now, if you could borrow at 2.2%, there would still be some appeal to RQI in the sense that leverage on someone else's balance sheet is always better than leverage on your balance sheet. But if you can borrow at 2.2% in this market, you can probably negotiate some Warren Buffett-style sweetheart preferred stock deals - do that. For us mere mortals who don't have billions and a huge name as negotiating leverage, that's a rate we can't touch.

It's a great deal. The only question is whether we want to have a portfolio of high quality REITs that is leveraged. Leverage increases yield and increases total return when prices are going up, but it also can increase downside if prices go down. In my market outlooks, I've been discussing how the S&P 500 is trading at a high valuation, but that high valuation is primarily among large-cap and growth stocks. REITs are all value stocks, and while a few are large-caps, the sector is full of small- to mid-caps. So yes, I want the leverage.

If you don't like leverage, sister fund Cohen & Steers Total Return Realty (RFI) has a very similar portfolio without significant leverage. However, it's also trading at a 3% premium, so I would put it on a watchlist rather than buying today.

Conclusion

RQI has achieved over its lifespan what many funds only dream of doing – beating its sector index in total returns:

It's doing this all while still trading at a discount to its NAV. A bargain and a winning one at that for income investors.

Will RQI outperform the market? Likely not over the long haul, as it's not exposed to massive growth giants focused on AI or the latest fad, but its stalwart reliability and income generation should not be passed over. Anyone seeking a reliable income from the market can readily enjoy cashing their distribution checks as they arrive without selling any of their shares, enjoying decades of reliable income with many more to follow.

For many retirees, they're not looking for the mathematically best exclusive return. It's because most of them don't want to hold on to their shares and do nothing with them. A large majority of retirees are forced to sell shares every single year to meet the requirements of their daily living expenses. This means that they are going to be minimizing the total return they would receive from a growth investment that does not pay them any distributions or dividends. For those individuals or for the other individuals who want to achieve the FIRE lifestyle, income from the market is going to be the most important aspect of their investment focus. An investment like RQI can provide them with highly reliable and highly valuable income for decades. It's something that many of these flash-in-the-pan growth style investments are not able to do because they do not pay reliable income.

That's the beauty of my Income Method. That's the beauty of income investing.

data.title: Lamar Advertising: High-Quality Billboard REIT With Strong Margins And Growth Potential
data.description:
Introduction & Financials

Lamar Advertising Company (NASDAQ:LAMR) is a REIT that’s the leader in US billboards, also having operations in Canada and expanding significantly over time through multiple acquisitions in more than 120 years since being founded.

Right now, the stock comes at a pretty attractive ~5.1% dividend yield and is able to deliver very strong margins, with the stock seemingly trading below a fair value even for relatively weak expectations, and with Warren Buffet’s Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B) recently taking a stake, let’s take a deeper look.

As they say, this is a very fragmented market (they have more total billboard faces than the next 10 competitors combined), while no customer accounts for more than 2% of their revenue, which is a very important aspect that shows very significant diversification, so definitely a plus especially since they don’t depend on tenants going bankrupt as much as a traditional REIT. If an advertiser has trouble, they can literally find a new one overnight in a normal environment for most of their locations. This industry is very “active” in this regard by nature, with many contracts generally being spread over seasons (e.g. the whole summer) or months (e.g. certain campaigns, promos, etc.) from my experience.

They trimmed the AFFO guidance to ~$8.1 to $8.2 per share in 2025 due to some one time expenses associated with their exit from the Vancouver contract where they were providing advertising services for TransLink's network, resulting in a midpoint reduction of ~$0.05.

That represents a 2% CAGR compared to 2024’s AFFO of $7.99 per share, bringing the CAGR since converting to a REIT in 2024 to 7%, which is very solid. Still, with about 102.65 million shares outstanding expected, that’s an AFFO of roughly ~$836.5 million in 2025, which is not bad for a $12.23 billion market cap, placing them at a P/AFFO ratio of around 14.75.

Financially, based on their latest report, we see a weak position when looking at the current assets and liabilities due mostly to the current maturities. As a recent update, they secured the refinancing back in late September when they issued $400 million due in 2033 (5.375% yield), with total refinancing transactions worth $1.1 billion thanks to also securing a $700 million 7-year Term Loan B facility (used to refinance the $600 million Term Loan B due in 2027) for 150 b.p. over SOFR. This significantly pushes their debt repayment schedule, with the company saying:

Collectively, the transactions, which are leverage neutral, will increase Lamar’s liquidity to more than $800 million, while reducing Lamar’s exposure to floating interest rates and extending the company’s debt maturity profile.

Dividends & Buybacks

They pay an okay dividend of ~5.1% now, with an 8.5% dividend per share CAGR over the years, generally trading at a 4% to 5% yield from the looks of it over the past decade. This is not bad if we add the company’s growth and especially their relative “uniqueness” in this market as the leader with very good margins.

Plus, they have a stock and debt buyback program going on, extended through March 31, 2026 and now including up to $400 million of Class A common stock (following a $150 million increase on May 15, 2025) and up to $250 million of outstanding debt, with $150 million already used to repurchase stock in the first half of 2025.

Risks & Opportunities

Something very important for a company like this will be rate cuts. This could boost advertising demand significantly, while also allowing the company to expand using cheaper debt and lower their interest expenses. Rate cuts could boost the demand for billboards since they’d revitalize the economy, which is ultimately the reason why the Fed uses them. With two more cuts expected after the September cut and potential to see this easing continue, we’re likely looking at a clear catalyst for advertising.

This can also be great news for REITs in particular, as it would lead to lower treasury yields. Rate cuts eventually lead to lower treasury yields (similar to SOFR), which means that the theoretical risk-free rate will drop, resulting in the market having to adjust their valuations for the new risk-adjusted results since the risk-free rate drops down, as I said before. This will then result in more demand especially for dividend stocks and REITs, as their relative safety, low leverage and high yields would result in more demand, potentially seeing the yield fall (as the stock rises) until the risk-adjusted level is deemed proper.

Also, thanks to rate cuts, the company’s AFFO could organically increase while they basically do nothing (other than refinancing), we’re looking at potentially higher dividends in the future once the company gets back on track and they feel confident in their financials again, as I said for most REITs before. This is still paying a decent 5.1% yield (I say decent because it’s only slightly above treasuries), but it could increase quite a bit since they have nearly $5 billion in debt.

Another point that LAMR highlights is that they are a massive player in a very fragmented market. They have a proven track record of M&As, announcing deals with Verde Outdoor Assets, Premier Outdoor Media, as well as smaller portfolios - and that's just over the past couple of years. As they also point out during the Q2 Earnings Q&A, this is a very fragmented market:

It's been an active year on the M&A front. Through Q2, we had spent $87 million in cash on 20 acquisitions, including a deal that we expect to close this morning, bringing the year-to-date total to approximately $110 million in cash acquisitions.

Also worth noting is that Warren Buffett’s Berkshire Hathaway bought roughly 1.2% of the company recently, which could bring some more attention to the stock. I generally don’t give much importance to these moves since these ~1.17 million shares are worth about $140 million, which is a very tiny amount for Berkshire and might be something Buffett is not even aware of - as far as I know, he’s mostly present for the larger deals nowadays, and will retire anyway. Still, having a team of smart people move forward with initiating this position could be a good indicator overall, especially since it brings more attention to the stock since the market pays a lot of attention to BRK’s moves.

Still, the environment can continue to show signs of weakness, and with gold breaching $4,000 for plenty of reasons, it’s likely that the market expects significant volatility and uncertainty - hence the need for a hedge in precious metals. Advertising revenue is ultimately something quite discretionary for companies, and economic weakness results in less spending, as we’ve seen before.

Although online advertising is dominating in terms of size and growth, the out-of-home/billboard market is still expected to increase at very solid rates, with studies suggesting even 8.1% CAGR from 2025 to 2030 globally, with segments like highway billboards dominating the market and accounting for ~26% of the revenue in 2024. Sure, digital is the future, and I’ve studied it extensively during my marketing degree back in uni (compared to barely a few motions for traditional marketing like billboards), but that doesn’t mean this will disappear.

If anything, it remains a very solid, reliable and cost-effective way of reaching a very wide audience in places where digital advertising has limitations. Yes, it’s not as targeted as digital (no cookies to reach the target audience you want), but it gives immense amounts of brand exposure and remains a good tool for advertisers to keep in mind.

I believe Lamar should also present interest in an international expansion, especially with rates falling and their valuation potentially improving, which could make issuing stock and debt more attractive and result in highly accretive deals.

Peer Comparison

Compared to their main peers - OUTFRONT Media (OUT) and Clear Channel Outdoor Holdings (CCO), and the Australian and European oOh!media Limited (OTCPK:OMLAF) and JCDecaux SE (OTCPK:JCDXF), we see the highest valuation from all points of view. This could show the general pressure this industry is in due to the market’s expectations around advertising spending. Still, as I said, LAMR could use this higher valuation to make accretive acquisitions, potentially expanding into Europe or Australia at a very good cost in case they use the stock, for example.

They also have the best margins in the industry, which is also worth remembering and arguably a reason behind the higher valuation.

Valuation

Assuming an AFFO of $836.5 million in 2025 (around the midpoint of their guidance), growing at a 4.5% CAGR over the first 5 years (below their performance since becoming a REIT, assuming positive effects from rate cuts improving the environment), 3.5% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years and 2.5% terminal growth rate. I’ll use a WACC of 7.5% (above my estimated 7.1% based on a cost of equity of 7.5% and cost of debt of 5.25%), 102.65 shares outstanding and $4.715 billion net debt based on their latest earnings report. This gets the Enterprise Value to $18.97 billion, and after deducting net debt, the equity value is $14.26 billion, leading to an intrinsic value per share of $138.91.

Conclusions

Overall, I rate Lamar Advertising a Buy now given their exposure and dividend yield, as well as the macro tailwinds potentially helping them continue to develop. The environment remains quite uncertain now and I’d prefer a better margin of safety, but they are also in a good position to improve significantly in case things go well, especially if they keep ramping it up with M&A activity.

data.title: Bristol Myers Squibb: Value Emerges From Distress
data.description:
For long term income seekers willing to wade through a period of market apathy on an emerging patent cliff, financial compression, and even execution risks, Bristol Myers Squibb (NYSE:BMY) offers a value entry point today that could benefit from a dividend yield as much as a total return appreciation, led by a bounce back in share prices. Where markets are seeing risks and competition, I see reasonable valuations in a company where transformation is already underway. BMY is a perfect valuation play for patient capital - the yield adds a buffer to a compelling Bull thesis.

Challenges and Transformation

The loss of exclusivity to Eliquis between 2026-28 is the single most concerning factor for BMY. With $3.7b in revenues coming in from Eliquis in Q2 2025, the annual run rate of $15b could easily be squeezed by $10b from generic competition. There are concerns about some components of the Growth portfolio as well, with Opdivo's patent expiration in 2028 also adding a $5b topline erosion possibility as well, although that is spread out further into the future. What more, there is market share erosion of other legacy products too, most notably Revlimid, the second biggest product after Eliquis.

BMY is fighting several battles at the same time. The Eliquis threat is more imminent and material. But an overall erosion in other legacy portfolio products is also concerning. The overall 1% topline growth in Q2 was primarily because of two significant and supportive factors, though. One, a ~10% growth in international markets papered over a slower US topline (down by ~3%). And two, the more encouraging trend - an 18% uptick in the growth portfolio.

Extrapolating the 18% growth in the growth portfolio for the next two years (middle of Eliquis patent cliff), BMY could actually make up for the $10b annual hole from Eliquis. Then make up for other patent cliffs coming up as well, most notably Opdivo, over the next two years. These are purely mathematical projections - in real market situations, execution risks will exist. Clinical setbacks like that seen with Cobenfy could set BMY back. Also, an expanding base could see a slowdown in growth in a lot of cases. However, the below table extrapolates current growth portfolio gains and shows how the patent cliff is not as damaging if execution holds up.

The above scenario applies simple extrapolation of growth and does a sanity check on possibilities like putting a cap on Breyanzi and Camzyos, as growth rates should moderate from the triple-digit levels today. Equally, other promising products like Cobenfy could grow much more than current levels that I have extrapolated. The broader point I am trying to establish through the extrapolation is that BMY's growth portfolio has the firepower to overcome patent cliffs in the near future from Eliquis and even in 2028 onwards due to growth portfolio patent cliffs like in Opdivo (a significant growth contributor today).

As per my extrapolation, whenever significant patent cliffs like Eliquis and Opdivo pan out, depending on the actual erosion, BMY might see higher single digit revenue erosion. But these drops are more than likely to be compensated by inroads made by the growth portfolio by the next year. In fact, by 2030, I see overall ~2% CAGR, through patent cliff troughs and rebounds, with no optionality from the pipeline baked in. That is not too far off for the average YoY growth rates seen in the past few years.

Valuations Conducive

Since the patent cliff and pipeline uncertainties introduce noise in the forward PE chart, I have used a revenue multiple valuation approach to check how BMY valuations have contracted in recent times. From the earlier levels of 4x EV to forward revenues in 2023 to ~3.5x even as recently as early 2025, the current valuations are towards an all time low of ~2.7x. This is a 25% discount from BMY's own 5-year average valuations.

The discount is, of course, bracing for uncertainties and execution risks going ahead. Revenue drops of the expected scale of higher single digits have happened to an extent in the past, most notably in March this year, where YoY revenues were down 6%. On an annual basis, revenues were down by ~2.5% in 2023 - around which time valuations touched the ~2.7x levels we see now.

I feel the current valuations are not going to react to the patent cliffs and revenue impacts standalone, going forward. The revenue estimates already bake in degrowth far more than what my extrapolation shows (~4-5% degrowth CAGR till 2030, compared to my more optimistic ~1-2% growth model). Given the pessimistic growth assumptions, valuations can be said to have baked in a far worse scenario than could happen as the growth portfolio continues delivering. The next few quarters will not look at the patent cliff but at how much the growth portfolio is able to plug the hole. For example, an earlier-than-expected impact of the patent cliff will impact headline numbers much more than expected. But valuations should react to other thesis altering factors like growth portfolio acceleration or a worse-than--anticipated patent cliff. Any other knee jerk de-rating will be an opportunity to accumulate.

Total Returns Potential

The forward dividend yield of 5.55% and a 17-year growth trajectory (~6.6% dividend growth in the past 5 years) looks very lucrative at current levels. A free cash flow yield of ~15% and a payout ratio of ~36% on net income also look reasonably well covered. The payout ratio looks aggressive when using GAAP metrics, but the free cash flow remains comfortable to maintain payouts, and even absorb some patent cliff related pressures. The $2.48 dividends paid in the trailing twelve months, when multiplied by the 2.04 outstanding shares, amounts to a little over $5b - that is, the free cash flow has 66% room to shrink to really start pressuring dividends.

Given the adequate dividend coverage and a 5-6% yield at cost, if investors buy BMY at current levels, the thesis is overtly bullish if BMY even manages to navigate the patent cliffs better than the 4-5% degrowth CAGR over the past 5 years. My simplistic extrapolation model signals that it is possible if the growth portfolio keeps delivering. BMY's immuno-oncology franchise should remain strong against competition. Any pipeline surprises and optionality are also going to help support valuations. According to BMY, the pipeline portfolio of 48 compounds across 40+ diseases is one of the best in the company's history. Several breakthrough use cases are expected through 2027, particularly on the Reblozyl, Camzyos, and radiopharmaceutical assets. This creates multiple catalysts for share price revaluation. I would expect the capital appreciation from the share price rebound to top the dividend yield by another 4-5% at least in the next 5 years on average. All in all, I think BMY is a reasonably valued Buy at this stage. Dividends are looking stable. Share prices should also rebound significantly for patient investors as BMY starts delivering beyond the built-in pessimistic scenarios that have dragged valuations to all time low.

data.title: Societe Generale: Solid Results, But Pricey Valuation (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Last year, we were buyers of Société Générale (OTCPK:SCGLY)(OTCPK:SCGLF) and reaffirmed the call in early February 2025; after a strong run (Fig. 1), we’re revisiting the name now on the heels of our BNP Paribas update and a tougher French policy backdrop. The Group has been through a tough spell, but a disciplined disposal program and tighter strategic focus are putting it back on track; our Buy rested on 1) a lower cost-to-income ratio evolution, 2) BoursoBank upside, 3) supportive results from the Investment Banking segment, 4) a solid capital buffer, and 5) Street consensus underestimation of the banking earnings.

At this stage, our team sees SocGen shares as fully valued; the disposal program and renewed domestic focus could prove more challenging to monetize amid France’s current turbulence. With the ECB already easing in 2025 (with a rate cut of 75 basis points), there is scope for further cuts into 2026, and the rate tailwind looks largely priced in.

Fig 1

H1 Results and Our Neutral Take

SocGen top-line sales reached €13.9 billion in H1, up +8.6%. With better cost management, the company reduced operating expenses by 2.6%, bringing the cost-to-income ratio to 64.4%. Compared to Wall Street consensus estimates, the company's Q2 profit before tax was 16% above, thanks to 2% more sales and 2% lower costs. Additionally, SocGen managed better loan losses. SocGen's ability to continue to improve its return on equity, so we are not surprised to see a share buyback announcement of €1 billion. Going to the cost of risk, the company recorded a CoR of 24 basis points in H1 (compared to 38 basis points from BNP Paribas). This is also below its 30 basis points of annual target. Net profit reached €3.1 billion with an RoTE at 10.3%. Regarding the solvency ratio, the company reported a CET 1 ratio of 13.5%, higher than the regulatory requirement.

Source: Société Générale Q2 results ppt - Fig 2

Going to the three divisions: French Retail & Insurance, International Retail, Mobility, and Global Banking & Investor Services, the company reported a broad-based beat and upgraded its Fiscal Year 2025 target for cost/income to <65% from <66% and ROTE to 9% from 8%. 2026 objectives will be revisited at FY25 results.

Fig 3

Why are we now Neutral?

  1. Taking advantage of our cross-coverage with BNP, banks are the economy’s thermometer; yet France’s political trajectory worries us. With a higher deficit expected, we believe the French government rating will be downgraded, which will provide a higher risk premium for equity (so, lower valuation). More importantly, this will likely result in more costly loans/mortgages for B2C and enterprises, weakening demand and consumption;
  2. Aside from the home-country ongoing challenges, credible dovish forces could nudge the ECB to ease rates again in the coming months. These are 1) U.S. tariffs impact with a stronger euro, and 2) any holdup in Germany’s fiscal stimulus;
  3. While we decided to be buyers in SocGen, the company had a disposal plan ahead of time. This is now well perceived by the market (and also partially executed). So, there is no positive surprise ahead;
  4. While we positively view SocGen's return of capital via buyback, this is not enough to justify a premium valuation compared to the sector. Last year, the bank traded at a P/E of 5.2x with a 0.33x TNAV compared to the EU banks' average of 6.5/7x P/E and TNAV between 0.8/1.1x. The bank now trades with a 0.82x TNAV (Fig. 4) with a lower RoTE as well as an aligned CET 1 ratio (Fig. 3). Even upgrading the EPS due to the buyback announcement, we see a 3-4% upside in a lower share count, still not enough to justify a premium price vs the sector.

Fig 4

Earnings Changes and Valuation

When we upgraded SocGen to Buy, we saw 1) a turn in domestic net interest income, 2) shareholders upside, 3) a disposal plan ahead with ongoing restructuring activities, and 4) EU stimulus to support near-term economic activity. Now, we are facing a tighter labor market, no domestic political stability, potential lower rates, and higher costs of risks. Additionally, Wall Street is now pricing SocGen correctly. While we see the company delivering a RoTE of 10% in 2026, with a capital sustainability above a 13% CET1 ratio, we find it hard to justify the current valuation. In 2026, assuming a net earnings of €5.2 billion (up from €4.5 billion in 2025), our EPS reached €6.8. This assumes net profit growth of 18%, including a lower share count. The bank trades at 8x P/E, well above the EU average, with a price-to-book value at 0.82x. P/E reflects an increased confidence in the achievability of sustained strong growth. In our prior note, we valued the stock at 6.5× P/E and 0.5× price to book value. Considering the low end of EU bank comps 7.0× P/E and 0.8× price to book value, SocGen's current fair value is estimated at €50 per share ($11.6 in ADR). This implied a neutral rating.

Risks

Downside risks include: 1) execution risks from the ongoing restructuring plan, 2) higher provision related to point 1, 3) weaker-than-anticipated interest rate, 4) lower sales in the IB and Asset Management division, 5) residual Russian exposure, 6) higher French retail pre-provision profits combined with higher impairments, and 7) lower capital returns story.

Conclusion

Compared to our last update, we see a full valuation on Société Générale and more downside risks than upside. For this reason, we moved our rating to an equal-weight rating. The French government context does not play any role in the domestic companies; however, on a fundamental basis, SocGen is well priced.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
